intro: Half marathon training (21K) is built on physiological principles: adaptation, overload, and recovery. Training intensity zones, weekly volume, and rest days drive your success. Use this free calculator to get a personalized training plan based on your fitness level—whether you're a beginner, intermediate, or advanced runner. It tells you exactly how many weeks to prepare for a 21K half marathon. Updated for 2026.
keyTakeaway: **Weeks = fitness level × training factor (minimum 12 weeks)**. Enter your fitness level and get a personalized half marathon (21K) training plan showing exactly how many weeks you need to prepare.

explanation:
Half marathon training combines science (physiology, biomechanics) with practical experience. Training zones, progressive overload, recovery, and nutrition form the complete system.

## How the calculation works

This calculator provides a personalized 21K training plan based on your fitness level. Whether you're a beginner, intermediate, or advanced runner, we calculate the ideal number of weeks to prepare for a half marathon. It's free and instant.

The calculator applies the training formula using your inputs, checking against typical ranges and alerting you if values fall outside reasonable limits.

## Final notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (training-related or medical), consult with a certified running coach or sports medicine professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: Why does fitness level affect training duration?
faq.a: Beginners need more time to build an aerobic base and adapt to running. Intermediate runners have a foundation and progress faster. Advanced runners need less time but still benefit from structured training.
Training blocks are phases: base building (aerobic foundation) → strength → speed work → tapering (recovery before race). Each phase builds on the last for peak race performance.
Tapering is the final 2-3 weeks before race day when you reduce training volume while keeping intensity. This lets your body recover and arrive fresh at the starting line.

faq.q: How many days per week should I train?
faq.a: Most half marathon runners train 4-6 days per week: 1-2 cross-training days (strength, flexibility, low-impact cardio) plus 3-4 running days mixing easy runs, tempo runs, and long runs.

faq.q: What should I do if I get injured?
faq.a: Go back 2 weeks to the training volume you could handle pain-free, then rebuild gradually. Cross-training (swimming, cycling) maintains fitness without impact. See a sports medicine professional if pain persists.
Yes, completely free. No registration needed. All calculations happen in your browser—we never store your data.
The results use standard, validated training formulas. They're an excellent reference guide, but every runner is different. For personalized guidance, consult a certified running coach.

faq.q: Does this calculator save my data?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen in your web browser. We don't send data anywhere or track your inputs.
